title: Cedarwood and bergamot soy candle
subtitle: A woody-citrus container candle in soy wax with a 6 to 7 cm jar
excerpt: Cedarwood essential oil provides a warm base note; bergamot adds a bright floral-citrus counterpoint with the characteristic Earl Grey quality. Together they produce a rounded, all-season fragrance that works in a sitting room or study. Soy container wax gives a clean, even burn.
sourceNotes: Fragrance blending ratios from Gracefruit fragrance-making guide (gracefruit.com, May 2026). Cedarwood and bergamot EO properties from Essential Oil Safety, Tisserand and Young (2014). Soy wax fragrance load rates from soy wax manufacturer datasheet.

recipe.yieldDescription: 1 container candle (~180 g wax in a 200 ml glass jar)
recipe.batchNotes: Double or triple for a batch of jars. Keep the total fragrance load at 8% of wax weight.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Cure for at least 3 days before burning. The cold scent throw from the unlit candle develops fully over the first week.
recipe.temperatureNote: Add the fragrance blend at 60 to 65 degrees Celsius. Bergamot is a volatile top note and evaporates more quickly than cedarwood if added too hot.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: 2 years. The cedarwood note is the more stable of the two; bergamot fades first as the lighter citrus molecules oxidise. Keep the lid on when not in use and store away from sunlight and heat.

glossaryTerms.definition: The percentage of fragrance or essential oil by weight relative to the wax weight. Soy container wax holds 8 to 10% fragrance load; this formula uses 8%.

The strength and reach of fragrance from a candle. Cold throw is the scent from an unlit candle; hot throw is the scent released during burning. Cedarwood has excellent hot throw; bergamot is primarily a cold throw note.

content.text: Cedarwood and bergamot balance well in a candle because they occupy different positions in the fragrance structure. Cedarwood is a base note with good tenacity in wax; it anchors the blend and carries through on the hot

. Bergamot is a top note and evaporates more readily; it lifts the blend on cold throw and on the first few minutes of burning. Adding the blend at 60 to 65 degrees Celsius preserves more of the bergamot than a hot pour. The 2:1 ratio (cedarwood to bergamot) keeps the base note dominant and prevents the citrus from reading as a cleaning product rather than a fragrance.

Bergamot essential oil: use bergapten-free (FCF) bergamot if you will handle the candle wax or liquid near the pour. Cold-pressed bergamot is phototoxic and should not contact skin before sun exposure. In a poured candle in a closed jar the contact risk is very low; FCF is the cautious choice regardless. The
for this recipe is 8% of wax weight.

items.prepNote: 5.5% of wax weight; the base note
2.8% of wax weight; bergapten-free preferred; adds the citrus note
CD-12 for a standard 6 to 7 cm jar

content.text: Fix the wick to the base of the jar and centre using a centring bar. Melt the soy wax in the pouring pitcher over a simmering-water bath, stirring occasionally. Remove from heat when fully liquid. Allow to cool to 60 to 65 degrees Celsius. Stir in the cedarwood and bergamot essential oils. Pour slowly into the prepared jar, keeping the wick upright. Leave undisturbed at room temperature for a minimum of 8 hours. Trim the wick to 5 mm before the first burn.

Allow the first burn to melt the wax all the way to the jar edge before extinguishing. For a 200 ml jar this takes around 3 hours. This sets the wax memory and prevents the candle from tunnelling on later burns.

2 years in a cool, dark place with the lid on. Bergamot fades before cedarwood as the lighter molecules evaporate; older candles will read as predominantly woody. The candle is still usable after the bergamot fades but the balance changes.
